---
title: Custom Dockerfiles
description: Use custom Dockerfiles instead of auto-generated ones for advanced build requirements
---

While Buildr automatically generates Dockerfiles for simple use cases, you can provide your own Dockerfile for advanced build requirements, multi-stage builds, or complex dependency management.

## Default Auto-Generated Dockerfile

By default, Buildr creates a simple Dockerfile based on your configuration:

```dockerfile
FROM python:{version}-alpine

COPY ./ /app/
RUN pip install --no-cache -r /app/requirements.txt

CMD python /app/{exec_file}
```

The template uses:
- `{version}`: Value from `PackageOptions.py_version` (default: 3)
- `{exec_file}`: Your script's filename

Source reference: containerize.py:38-45

### Limitations of Auto-Generation

The default Dockerfile works well for simple applications but has limitations:

- Uses Alpine Linux (small but may have compatibility issues)
- Installs all dependencies from `requirements.txt` without caching optimization
- No support for system packages (apt, apk)
- No multi-stage builds
- No custom build arguments or environment variables
- Limited to Python applications

## Using a Custom Dockerfile

To use your own Dockerfile, add the `dockerfile` parameter to `PackageOptions`:

```python
from metaparticle_pkg import Containerize

@Containerize(
    package={
        'repository': 'myusername',
        'name': 'my-app',
        'dockerfile': './Dockerfile'  # Path to your custom Dockerfile
    }
)
def main():
    print("Using custom Dockerfile!")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

<Tip>
The `dockerfile` parameter accepts any valid file path. Buildr copies your Dockerfile to the build context before building.
</Tip>

Source reference: containerize.py:33-36

## Custom Dockerfile Examples

### Example 1: Multi-Stage Build

Optimize image size with multi-stage builds:

```dockerfile
# Build stage
FROM python:3.11-slim as builder

WORKDIR /app

# Install build dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    gcc \
    python3-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Python dependencies
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --user --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Runtime stage
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Copy only the installed packages from builder
COPY --from=builder /root/.local /root/.local
COPY . .

# Make sure scripts in .local are usable
ENV PATH=/root/.local/bin:$PATH

CMD ["python", "app.py"]
```

```python
@Containerize(
    package={
        'repository': 'myusername',
        'name': 'optimized-app',
        'dockerfile': './Dockerfile'
    }
)
def main():
    # Your application code
    pass
```

### Example 2: System Dependencies

Install system packages required by your application:

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    postgresql-client \
    libpq-dev \
    curl \
    git \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Python dependencies
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Create non-root user
RUN useradd -m -u 1000 appuser && chown -R appuser:appuser /app
USER appuser

CMD ["python", "app.py"]
```

```python
@Containerize(
    package={
        'repository': 'myusername',
        'name': 'postgres-app',
        'dockerfile': './Dockerfile'
    },
    runtime={'ports': [5000]}
)
def main():
    import psycopg2
    # Your database application
    pass
```

### Example 3: Layer Caching Optimization

Maximize Docker layer caching for faster rebuilds:

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Copy only requirements first (cached unless requirements change)
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code (changes frequently, cached separately)
COPY . .

CMD ["python", "app.py"]
```

<Warning>
The auto-generated Dockerfile copies all files first, then installs dependencies. This invalidates the cache on any file change. Custom Dockerfiles let you optimize caching.
</Warning>

### Example 4: Development vs Production

Use build arguments for environment-specific builds:

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Build argument for environment
ARG ENVIRONMENT=production

COPY requirements.txt .

# Install production or dev dependencies
RUN if [ "$ENVIRONMENT" = "development" ]; then \
        pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt && \
        pip install --no-cache-dir pytest black flake8; \
    else \
        pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt; \
    fi

COPY . .

# Different entrypoints based on environment
CMD if [ "$ENVIRONMENT" = "development" ]; then \
        python -m flask run --host=0.0.0.0 --debug; \
    else \
        gunicorn -w 4 -b 0.0.0.0:8000 app:app; \
    fi
```

```python
import os
from metaparticle_pkg import Containerize

env = os.getenv('ENVIRONMENT', 'production')

@Containerize(
    package={
        'repository': 'myusername',
        'name': f'my-app-{env}',
        'dockerfile': './Dockerfile'
    },
    runtime={'ports': [8000 if env == 'production' else 5000]}
)
def main():
    # Your application
    pass
```

<Tip>
You cannot pass build args directly through Buildr. Instead, modify your Dockerfile to use environment variables or create separate Dockerfiles for different environments.
</Tip>

### Example 5: Non-Python Base Images

Use any base image, not just Python:

```dockerfile
# Use a Node.js base for a hybrid app
FROM node:18-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install Python
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    python3 \
    python3-pip \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Node dependencies
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --production

# Install Python dependencies
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip3 install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application
COPY . .

CMD ["python3", "app.py"]
```

```python
@Containerize(
    package={
        'repository': 'myusername',
        'name': 'hybrid-app',
        'dockerfile': './Dockerfile'
    }
)
def main():
    # Python app that uses Node.js tooling
    pass
```

## Dockerfile Location and Build Context

### Build Context

Buildr builds from the current directory (`.`) by default:

```python
# Directory structure:
# .
# ├── app.py
# ├── requirements.txt
# ├── Dockerfile
# └── modules/
#     └── helper.py
```

All files in the current directory are available to `COPY` in your Dockerfile.

Source reference: builder/docker_builder.py:13-21

### Relative Paths

The `dockerfile` parameter can be a relative or absolute path:

<Tabs>
  <Tab title="Same directory">
    ```python
    @Containerize(
        package={
            'repository': 'myusername',
            'dockerfile': './Dockerfile'
        }
    )
    def main():
        pass
    ```
  </Tab>
  
  <Tab title="Subdirectory">
    ```python
    @Containerize(
        package={
            'repository': 'myusername',
            'dockerfile': './docker/Dockerfile.prod'
        }
    )
    def main():
        pass
    ```
  </Tab>
  
  <Tab title="Absolute path">
    ```python
    @Containerize(
        package={
            'repository': 'myusername',
            'dockerfile': '/home/user/dockerfiles/Dockerfile'
        }
    )
    def main():
        pass
    ```
  </Tab>
</Tabs>

### .dockerignore

Create a `.dockerignore` file to exclude files from the build context:

```text
# .dockerignore
__pycache__
*.pyc
*.pyo
*.pyd
.Python
env/
venv/
.git
.gitignore
.vscode/
.idea/
*.md
tests/
.pytest_cache/
.coverage
*.log
```

This reduces build context size and speeds up builds.

## Requirements.txt

Even with custom Dockerfiles, ensure `requirements.txt` exists:

```text
# requirements.txt
flask==2.3.0
requests==2.31.0
gunicorn==21.2.0
psycopg2-binary==2.9.9
```

Your custom Dockerfile should install these dependencies:

```dockerfile
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
```

<Warning>
The auto-generated Dockerfile expects `requirements.txt` at the root. Custom Dockerfiles can reference it from any location.
</Warning>

## Debugging Custom Dockerfiles

If your build fails, enable verbose output:

```python
@Containerize(
    package={
        'repository': 'myusername',
        'dockerfile': './Dockerfile',
        'verbose': True  # Show detailed build output
    }
)
def main():
    pass
```

This displays all Docker build steps and helps identify issues.

Source reference: builder/docker_builder.py:34-58

### Common Build Errors

<AccordionGroup>
  <Accordion title="Dockerfile not found">
    ```
    Error: Dockerfile not found at path: ./Dockerfile
    ```
    
    **Solution:** Verify the path is correct and the file exists:
    ```bash
    ls -la ./Dockerfile
    ```
  </Accordion>
  
  <Accordion title="COPY failed">
    ```
    COPY failed: stat /var/lib/docker/tmp/.../file: no such file or directory
    ```
    
    **Solution:** Ensure files being copied exist in the build context (current directory).
  </Accordion>
  
  <Accordion title="Package not found">
    ```
    ERROR: Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement package-name
    ```
    
    **Solution:** Check `requirements.txt` for typos and ensure the package exists on PyPI.
  </Accordion>
  
  <Accordion title="Permission denied">
    ```
    Permission denied while trying to connect to Docker daemon socket
    ```
    
    **Solution:** Ensure Docker daemon is running and your user has permissions:
    ```bash
    sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
    # Log out and back in
    ```
  </Accordion>
</AccordionGroup>

## Best Practices

<Steps>
  <Step title="Use specific base image tags">
    Avoid `latest` tags for reproducible builds:
    
    ```dockerfile
    # Good
    FROM python:3.11.5-slim
    
    # Avoid
    FROM python:latest
    ```
  </Step>
  
  <Step title="Minimize layers">
    Combine RUN commands to reduce image size:
    
    ```dockerfile
    # Good
    RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
        pkg1 pkg2 pkg3 \
        && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
    
    # Avoid
    RUN apt-get update
    RUN apt-get install -y pkg1
    RUN apt-get install -y pkg2
    ```
  </Step>
  
  <Step title="Use .dockerignore">
    Exclude unnecessary files from the build context to speed up builds.
  </Step>
  
  <Step title="Run as non-root">
    Create and use a non-root user for security:
    
    ```dockerfile
    RUN useradd -m -u 1000 appuser
    USER appuser
    ```
  </Step>
  
  <Step title="Optimize layer caching">
    Copy dependencies before application code:
    
    ```dockerfile
    COPY requirements.txt .
    RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
    COPY . .
    ```
  </Step>
</Steps>

## Complete Example: Production-Ready Dockerfile

Here's a comprehensive custom Dockerfile with all best practices:

```dockerfile
# Multi-stage build for optimized image size
FROM python:3.11.5-slim as builder

WORKDIR /app

# Install build dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    gcc \
    python3-dev \
    libpq-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Python dependencies with caching
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --user --no-cache-dir --no-warn-script-location \
    -r requirements.txt

# Runtime stage
FROM python:3.11.5-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install runtime dependencies only
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    libpq5 \
    curl \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy installed packages from builder
COPY --from=builder /root/.local /root/.local

# Create non-root user
RUN useradd -m -u 1000 appuser && \
    chown -R appuser:appuser /app

# Copy application code
COPY --chown=appuser:appuser . .

# Set PATH to include user-installed packages
ENV PATH=/root/.local/bin:$PATH

# Switch to non-root user
USER appuser

# Health check
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --start-period=5s --retries=3 \
    CMD curl -f http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1

# Run application
CMD ["gunicorn", "-w", "4", "-b", "0.0.0.0:8000", "app:app"]
```

```python
from metaparticle_pkg import Containerize

@Containerize(
    package={
        'repository': 'mycompany',
        'name': 'production-app',
        'dockerfile': './Dockerfile',
        'publish': True,
        'verbose': False  # Clean output in CI/CD
    },
    runtime={
        'executor': 'metaparticle',
        'replicas': 5,
        'ports': [8000],
        'public': True
    }
)
def main():
    # Your production application
    from app import app
    app.run()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

## When to Use Custom Dockerfiles

Use custom Dockerfiles when you need:

<CardGroup cols={2}>
  <Card title="System packages" icon="box">
    Install OS-level dependencies like databases, compilers, or tools
  </Card>
  
  <Card title="Multi-stage builds" icon="layer-group">
    Optimize image size by separating build and runtime stages
  </Card>
  
  <Card title="Security hardening" icon="shield">
    Run as non-root user, minimize attack surface
  </Card>
  
  <Card title="Build optimization" icon="rocket">
    Maximize layer caching for faster rebuilds
  </Card>
  
  <Card title="Complex dependencies" icon="puzzle-piece">
    Handle C extensions, compiled code, or native libraries
  </Card>
  
  <Card title="Custom base images" icon="cubes">
    Use company-specific or non-Python base images
  </Card>
</CardGroup>
